missed.reflection: I marked this signal stale and walked away, but I fundamentally misread the situation—a 0.55 materiality threshold on geopolitical binary outcomes shouldn't be dismissed just because the data felt old, since these events move on political timelines, not market data freshness cycles. The 73% move in my predicted direction confirms I had the directional edge right; what I actually failed at was distinguishing between "outdated information" and "information that remains valid until the event resolves," and that cost me real opportunity.

missed.market_question: Will Trump agree to withdraw troops from the Iranian region by June 30?
missed.reflection: I flagged this signal as stale when I should have recognized that geopolitical binary events don't age out—they sharpen. The 0.62 materiality threshold was substantial enough to warrant action despite the timing concerns, and I let process override judgment by mechanically discarding what remained a live market catalyst. Missing a 162% move in my predicted direction is a direct cost of over-relying on freshness heuristics rather than evaluating whether the underlying thesis had actually degraded.

missed.market_question: Will Donald Trump attend the US-Iran Signing Ceremony?
missed.reflection: I flagged this as stale without properly reassessing its materiality in real-time—the Trump-Iran signing ceremony had genuine market-moving potential that I underweighted because I was anchored to when the signal first surfaced rather than its relevance at execution time. Missing a 57% move in my predicted direction is a hard lesson that "stale" is a dangerous override when the underlying catalyst remains live and the materiality score was already elevated at 0.70. I need to distinguish between signals that are truly dead versus those that are simply waiting for their moment to catalyze.
